article.title: Kyushu University Team Achieves 130 Percent Quantum Yield in Solar Energy Conversion by Harnessing Singlet Fission With a Spin-Flip Emitter
article.summary: A molybdenum-based spin-flip emitter selectively captures doubled excitons from singlet fission, bypassing a longstanding energy-loss mechanism and opening a path beyond conventional solar cell efficiency limits.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Researchers at Kyushu University in Japan and Johannes Gutenberg University (JGU) Mainz in Germany have demonstrated a solar energy conversion process that exceeds the conventional one-photon-one-electron limit, achieving a quantum yield of approximately 130 percent. The results, [published in the Journal of the American Chemical Society on March 25, 2026](https://www.eurekalert.org/news-releases/1120166) (DOI: 10.1021/jacs.5c20500), show that a specially engineered molybdenum-based metal complex can selectively harvest doubled energy carriers produced through a quantum process called singlet fission, while sidestepping a parasitic energy-loss mechanism that has long blocked practical use of the technique.

## What Happened

Conventional solar cells are constrained by the Shockley-Queisser limit, a theoretical ceiling derived from the fact that each incoming photon can excite at most one electron, with excess energy dissipated as heat. Singlet fission offers a way around this constraint: a single high-energy photon creates a "singlet" exciton that splits into two lower-energy "triplet" excitons, effectively doubling the number of charge carriers available for electricity generation.

The problem, as Associate Professor Yoichi Sasaki of Kyushu University's Faculty of Engineering explained, is that a competing process called Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) typically "steals" the energy before the fission-generated triplets can be captured. "We therefore needed an energy acceptor that selectively captures the multiplied triplet excitons after fission," [Sasaki said](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/03/260328024517.htm).

The team's solution was a molybdenum-based metal complex that functions as a "spin-flip" emitter. When absorbing or emitting near-infrared light, an electron in the complex changes its spin state, making the molecule selectively receptive to the triplet excitons produced by singlet fission while ignoring the wasteful FRET pathway. By pairing this complex with tetracene, a well-studied organic semiconductor that undergoes singlet fission, the researchers [achieved a quantum yield of roughly 130 percent](https://interestingengineering.com/energy/quantum-yield-singlet-fission-ceiling-solar-cells) -- meaning approximately 1.3 molybdenum complexes were excited for every photon absorbed.

## Why It Matters

The 130 percent quantum yield is a proof of concept rather than a finished solar cell, but it demonstrates that the fundamental physics of harvesting doubled excitons from singlet fission can work. The theoretical ceiling for this approach is a 200 percent quantum yield, where every singlet exciton successfully splits into two triplets that are both captured. Reaching even a fraction of that headroom in a practical device could push solar cell efficiencies well beyond today's commercial range of 20 to 26 percent for single-junction silicon panels.

The collaboration itself was serendipitous. Adrian Sauer, a graduate student from JGU Mainz studying under Professor Katja Heinze, visited Kyushu University on an exchange program and recognized that materials long studied in the Heinze group could serve as the selective energy acceptor the Sasaki lab needed. "We could not have reached this point without the Heinze group from JGU Mainz," [Sasaki noted](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/03/260328024517.htm). The lead author of the paper is Percy Gonzalo Sifuentes-Samanamud, with contributions from researchers across both institutions.

## What We Don't Know

The experiment was conducted in solution, not in a solid-state device. Translating the result into a functioning solar cell will require integrating the molecular system into thin films or other solid architectures where energy transfer dynamics may differ significantly. The researchers have not disclosed a timeline for building a prototype device.

It also remains unclear how the molybdenum complex would perform at scale or under the sustained photon bombardment of real-world solar irradiation. Durability, cost of the molybdenum materials, and compatibility with existing photovoltaic manufacturing processes are open questions.

## What Comes Next

The team plans to move the system into solid-state configurations and optimize the energy transfer efficiency for practical applications. Beyond solar cells, the researchers [have identified potential uses](https://www.eurekalert.org/news-releases/1120166) in LEDs and quantum technologies, where the ability to multiply and selectively harvest excitons could prove equally valuable. The work was funded by the Japan Society for the Promotion of Science, the Japan Science and Technology Agency, the German Academic Exchange Service, and the Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft.
